result:
The cursor blinks. That's where everyone thinks writing starts, but it isn't. Writing starts in the body—a tension in the jaw, a half-thought caught between exhalations, the static of a morning that hasn't decided what it wants to be yet.

I'm thinking about my grandmother's hands. She's been gone eleven years and I still see them: knuckles like river stones, the wedding ring she never took off even when her fingers swelled in summer. She used to peel apples in one long ribbon, and I'd watch the green spiral fall into the sink while she told me about the war, or about her sister who married a man from Trieste, or about nothing at all. The talking and the peeling were the same activity. Both made something whole into something usable.

I think we've lost that—the patience of one continuous motion. Everything now is interruption disguised as efficiency. We peel the apple in seventeen separate sessions, checking our phones between each cut, and at the end we have apple slices but no story.

Outside my window a crow is arguing with another crow about something important. Crows have a syntax we don't understand. They have funerals, did you know that? When one of them dies, the others gather around the body and make a noise that isn't quite mourning but isn't not mourning either. They're trying to figure out what happened. They're conducting an inquest in a language older than language.

I want to tell you about a moment last Tuesday. I was at the grocery store and an old man dropped a jar of olives. It shattered. Brine and green orbs rolled across the linoleum. He stood there for a long second, looking down, and I watched his face do something complicated—not quite embarrassment, not quite grief, but something in between, as if the jar had been a final straw he didn't know he was carrying. Then a teenager in an apron came over with a mop and said, "Don't even worry about it, sir, happens all the time," and the old man's whole face softened, and I almost cried in aisle six because kindness is the most underrated form of resistance.

We're told the world is ending. Maybe it is. Maybe it always is. Empires fall, ice melts, languages die, my grandmother died, the crow on my windowsill will die, and someday this paragraph will be unreadable because the language it's written in will have shifted into something else, the way Latin shifted into Italian and French and Spanish and Portuguese, slowly, then all at once. But people will still drop olives. People will still hand each other mops. The small grammar of being alive together will continue in some form, even when the larger sentences fall apart.

I read once that the Japanese have a word, *komorebi*, for the way sunlight filters through leaves. I love that we name things like this. I love that someone, somewhere, looked up and decided this deserves a word. Not the leaves, not the sun, but the relationship between them. The verb of light meeting chlorophyll. There must be a word for the look on the old man's face. There must be a word for the way grandmother-hands spiral apple peels. There must be a word for the static of a morning that hasn't decided what it wants to be.

If there isn't, we should make one.

The crow is gone now. It got bored of arguing or it won the argument or the other crow won and it left in disgust. I'll never know. The not-knowing is fine. Most of life is not-knowing. The story we tell ourselves—that we understand our own motivations, that we know why we love who we love, that we chose this job, this city, this hour of being awake when we should be sleeping—is a kind of beautiful lie we agree to maintain. Underneath, we're all just walking around in weather we didn't predict, doing our best with the tools we have, occasionally dropping the jar.

I have maybe a hundred words left. What do I do with them? I want to say something true, but truth is slippery, and the closer I get to it the more it dissolves into qualifications. So I'll say something smaller. I'll say: pay attention. Not to everything—you can't, no one can—but to one thing, today, fully. The way someone says your name. The taste of water when you're actually thirsty. The fact that you exist at all, which is statistically absurd and emotionally enormous and somehow we keep forgetting.

Forty words.

I don't know who's reading this. Maybe you. Maybe no one. Maybe a future version of myself who has forgotten that I wrote it. Hello, whoever you are. I hope you got to peel something today in one long ribbon. I hope someone handed you a mop.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
